groups.label: Backend Systems & Architecture
groups.description: The strongest backend-facing work: service architecture, APIs, concurrency, and integration-heavy systems.

groups.label: Applied Data & Intelligent Systems
groups.description: Projects where data processing, AI workflows, or prediction logic make the system meaningfully more interesting.

groups.label: Play / Games
groups.description: Smaller game builds and interactive experiments that show playfulness, graphics instincts, and a willingness to build for feel as well as function.

items.title: Pantheon (WIP)
items.summary: A unified game-library backend designed as a modular monolith with explicit architecture boundaries and real-time event delivery.
items.impact: A modular-monolith backend for game libraries, built around clean boundaries, live updates, and maintainable growth.
notes: Clean architecture, live updates, and serious engineering discipline in one backend.
The interesting part here is not just the stack, but the discipline around keeping the system easy to evolve as features and integrations grow.

items.title: Stock Portfolio Management
items.summary: A secure REST backend for portfolio tracking with authentication, persistence, and live market data integration.
items.impact: A secure portfolio-tracking backend with auth, persistence, and real-time market integration.
notes: Secure, documented, and practical backend work with real integrations.
It is a good example of the kind of backend work I enjoy most: practical APIs, clear contracts, and systems that feel usable rather than purely academic.

items.title: Image WebCrawler
items.summary: A multi-threaded crawler that recursively explores in-domain pages and extracts images while controlling duplication and resource usage.
items.impact: A concurrent image crawler that recursively scans in-domain pages while controlling duplicates and resource usage.
notes: Concurrency-heavy systems work that is instantly more memorable than another CRUD app.
It is one of my more systems-heavy projects, and a good example of enjoying problems that are as much about behavior and constraints as they are about features.

items.title: Uni-link
items.summary: A backend and integration layer for a unified social-media management workflow across multiple platforms.
items.impact: An integration-focused backend that unifies social-platform workflows behind one service layer.
notes: A useful integration backend that proves I can build beyond the JVM comfort zone.
It also adds a useful change of texture to the portfolio by showing product-facing backend work outside my usual Java-heavy lane.

items.title: Stonks
items.summary: A stock prediction application that combines social-media sentiment with historical market data to estimate future movement.
items.impact: A data-heavy web app that combines market history and social sentiment to explore stock prediction.
notes: Finance, NLP, and experimentation brought together in one opinionated project.
It sits in the more experimental part of my work, but still leans on the same instincts around pipelines, data boundaries, and usefulness.

items.title: Telegram GPT Bot
items.summary: A multilingual Telegram bot that uses text and voice workflows to make AI assistance more accessible in lower-access settings.
items.impact: A multilingual AI bot that blends messaging, voice, and translation into one accessible workflow.
notes: Applied AI with an actual point of view, not just a chatbot wrapper.
It is one of the more human-centered pieces in the set, which is part of what makes it memorable to talk about.

items.title: Insta-Diagnosis
items.summary: A web application for disease prediction using medical scans, secure record handling, and image pre-processing before model inference.
items.impact: A healthcare-focused web app that combines image preprocessing, model inference, and secure records.
notes: Computer vision in a healthcare setting, with real product and security context.
Even as a smaller project, it reflects a recurring theme in my work: trying to make technically ambitious ideas concrete and usable.

items.title: Atari Asteroids
items.summary: A classic arcade-style Asteroids recreation built as a game-focused programming exercise around movement, collision, and loop design.
items.impact: A playable Asteroids remake focused on motion, collision, and arcade feel.
notes: A pure feel-first build that shows I like engineering for interaction, not just infrastructure.
It is a different kind of engineering problem, but still one that rewards clarity, iteration, and attention to behavior.

items.title: Sky Jump
items.summary: A 2D platformer built in Python around level progression, enemy behavior, and gameplay pacing.
items.impact: A Python platformer built around progression, enemy behavior, and moment-to-moment game feel.
notes: A more visual project that still rewards systems thinking and careful tuning.
The visual side matters here more than in my backend work, which is exactly why it is a useful contrast project.

items.title: Snake Game
items.summary: A compact Java implementation of the classic Snake game, packaged and runnable as a desktop jar.
items.impact: A compact desktop Snake build that packages classic gameplay into a clean end-to-end Java project.
notes: Small, finished, and runnable, which is part of the appeal.
Not every project needs to be huge to be worth keeping around if it shows a genuine pattern in how you like to build.
